source.title: Striatal indirect pathway mediates exploration via collicular competition

confidence_rationale: High-impact Nature paper with optogenetic dissection of indirect pathway function in a licking task. Shows that indirect SPNs suppress ongoing actions and enable exploration via inter-collicular competition -- a concrete mechanism for how the indirect pathway mediates the go/no-go arbitration that is central to Q-016. Very direct mechanistic evidence. Confidence reduced by species (mouse) and simplicity of the lateralized licking task compared to tri-loop conflict.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Indirect striatal projection neurons (iSPNs) suppress ongoing contraversive actions and promote ipsiversive exploration via modulation of superior colliculus, mediated by inter-collicular competition where each hemisphere inhibits the other. iSPN inactivation impairs suppression of devalued actions and reduces exploratory licking.
mapping.ree_translation: In REE's tri-loop architecture, the indirect pathway plays the role of the no-go gate. This paper shows that the indirect pathway does not merely suppress -- it actively mediates competition between alternative action representations downstream (via SC). For Q-016, this means that the arbitration policy must preserve the competitive structure downstream: a pure winner-take-all policy that silences the indirect pathway's influence would eliminate exploration. The collicular competition mechanism suggests that the arbitration policy needs to be soft-competitive rather than hard-suppressive -- otherwise the losing gate collapses entirely rather than merely yielding priority, and coupling collapse follows.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The collicular competition mechanism is specific to the oculomotor/action-direction domain in a simple lateralized licking task. REE's tri-loop conflict involves loops at cognitive, limbic, and motor levels simultaneously -- there is no evidence from this paper that the same collicular competition mechanism operates across all three REE loops. The exploration function studied here (devalued-action suppression and exploratory behavior) is behaviorally distinct from the REE tri-loop conflict scenario where ethical and cognitive loops may disagree with a motor loop.
mapping.source_context: Mouse optogenetics, in vivo electrophysiology, lateralized licking task, healthy adult mice

confidence_components.notes: Exceptionally clean optogenetic dissection in top-tier journal. Transfer risk primarily from species and from the simplicity of the lateralized licking task -- REE tri-loop conflict involves interactions across qualitatively different loop types.
